content:
# Numbers Chapter 13 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 162-164 | Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0121-0122 (LXX)
**Source Images:** `NUM_013_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_013_page_002.jpg`, `NUM_013_page_003.jpg`; `NUM_013_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_013_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### YHWH Commands the Sending of Spies (vv. 1-3)

**1** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

**2** "Send for yourself men, and let them spy out the land of Canaan, which I am giving to the Sons of Israel. One man, one man from each tribe of his fathers you shall send, every one a leader among them."

**3** And Moses sent them from the wilderness of Paran, according to the mouth of YHWH. All of them were men who were heads of the Sons of Israel.

---

### The Names of the Twelve Spies (vv. 4-16)

**4** And these were their names: from the tribe of Reuben, Shammua son of Zaccur;

**5** from the tribe of Simeon, Shaphat son of Hori;

**6** from the tribe of Judah, Caleb son of Jephunneh;

**7** from the tribe of Issachar, Igal son of Joseph;

**8** from the tribe of Ephraim, Hoshea son of Nun;

**9** from the tribe of Benjamin, Palti son of Raphu;

**10** from the tribe of Zebulun, Gaddiel son of Sodi;

**11** from the tribe of Joseph, that is, the tribe of Manasseh, Gaddi son of Susi;

**12** from the tribe of Dan, Ammiel son of Gemalli;

**13** from the tribe of Asher, Sethur son of Michael;

**14** from the tribe of Naphtali, Nahbi son of Vophsi;

**15** from the tribe of Gad, Geuel son of Machi.

**16** These are the names of the men whom Moses sent to spy out the land. And Moses called Hoshea son of Nun, Joshua.

---

### Moses' Instructions to the Spies (vv. 17-20)

**17** And Moses sent them to spy out the land of Canaan, and he said to them, "Go up this way into the Negev, and go up into the hill country.

**18** And you shall see the land, what it is, and the people who dwell in it, whether they are strong or weak, whether they are few or many.

**19** And what is the land in which they dwell, whether it is good or bad; and what are the cities in which they dwell, whether in camps or in fortifications.

**20** And what is the land, whether it is fertile or lean, whether there is wood in it or not. And you shall be strong and take from the fruit of the land." And the days were the days of the first-ripe grapes.

---

### The Spies Traverse the Land (vv. 21-24)

**21** And they went up and spied out the land from the wilderness of Zin unto Rehob, at the entrance of Hamath.

**22** And they went up into the Negev and came unto Hebron. And Ahiman, Sheshai, and Talmai, the descendants of Anak, were there. And Hebron was built seven years before Zoan of Egypt.

**23** And they came unto the Valley of Eshcol, and they cut down from there a branch with one cluster of grapes, and they carried it on a pole between two men, and also some of the pomegranates and some of the figs.

**24** That place was called the Valley of Eshcol, on account of the cluster which the Sons of Israel cut down from there.

---

### The Return and Report (vv. 25-29)

**25** And they returned from spying out the land at the end of forty days.

**26** And they went and came to Moses and to Aaron and to all the congregation of the Sons of Israel, to the wilderness of Paran, to Kadesh. And they brought back word to them and to all the congregation, and they showed them the fruit of the land.

**27** And they told him and said, "We came to the land to which you sent us, and indeed it flows with milk and honey, and this is its fruit.

**28** However, the people who dwell in the land are strong, and the cities are fortified and very great. And also the descendants of Anak we saw there.

**29** Amalek dwells in the land of the Negev, and the Hittite and the Jebusite and the Amorite dwell in the hill country, and the Canaanite dwells by the sea and along the Jordan."

---

### Caleb's Dissent and the Evil Report (vv. 30-33)

**30** And Caleb silenced the people before Moses and said, "Let us surely go up and possess it, for we are well able to overcome it."

**31** But the men who had gone up with him said, "We are not able to go up against the people, for they are stronger than we."

**32** And they brought out an evil report of the land which they had spied out to the Sons of Israel, saying, "The land through which we passed to spy it out is a land that devours its inhabitants, and all the people whom we saw in its midst are men of great stature.

**33** And there we saw the Nephilim — the sons of Anak, who come from the Nephilim — and we were in our own eyes as grasshoppers, and so we were in their eyes."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Textual Notes

1. **Verse 1 -- "And YHWH spoke to Moses" (וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה):** The chapter opens with the standard prophetic reception formula. A significant tension exists between the MT and the LXX regarding the agency of the mission. In the MT, YHWH directly commands the sending of the spies ("Send for yourself men"). In Deuteronomy 1:22-23, however, Moses attributes the initiative to the people ("And all of you came near to me and said, 'Let us send men before us'"), with Moses approving the plan. The LXX of Numbers 13 follows the MT here but shows awareness of the Deuteronomic parallel. The two accounts are not contradictory: the people proposed, Moses approved, and YHWH commanded -- a threefold authorization. The phrase lekha ("for yourself") in verse 2 may carry the nuance "at your discretion" or "for your benefit," as in lekh-lekha (Genesis 12:1), suggesting YHWH grants Moses latitude in the execution.

2. **Verse 2 -- "One man, one man from each tribe" (אִישׁ אֶחָד אִישׁ אֶחָד לְמַטֵּה אֲבֹתָיו):** The distributive repetition ish echad ish echad (one man, one man) is a Hebrew idiom for "one from each." The requirement that each be a nasi (leader, prince, chieftain) among them elevates the reconnaissance from a military scouting party to a national delegation representing all twelve tribes. The LXX renders ανδρα ενα κατα φυλην (one man per tribe), smoothing the Hebrew repetition. The word matteh (tribe, staff) literally means "rod" or "branch," derived from the staff of authority associated with tribal leadership. The phrase matteh avotav (tribe of his fathers) emphasizes patrilineal descent and ancestral territory claims.

3. **Verse 3 -- "from the wilderness of Paran" (מִמִּדְבַּר פָּארָן):** The departure point connects directly to 12:16, where the camp moved from Hazeroth to the wilderness of Paran. The phrase al pi YHWH (according to the mouth of YHWH) confirms divine authorization. The LXX adds that they departed "through the wilderness of Pharan" (δια της ερημου Φαραν). Paran is identified with the northeastern Sinai plateau, south of the Negev, placing the Israelite camp in a staging area from which the Negev route northward into Canaan was the natural approach.

4. **Verse 4 -- The Tribal List (vv. 4-15):** The list of twelve spies follows a unique order that differs from every other tribal enumeration in the Pentateuch. It begins with Reuben (the firstborn) but then follows neither birth order, nor the camp arrangement of Numbers 2, nor the census order of Numbers 1. Notably, Judah appears third (v. 6) and provides one of the two faithful spies (Caleb), while Ephraim appears fifth (v. 8) and provides the other (Joshua/Hoshea). The tribe of Levi is absent (having no territorial allotment), and the tribe of Joseph is divided into Ephraim (v. 8) and Manasseh (v. 11) as in all census lists. The LXX order diverges significantly from the MT, placing the tribes in a different sequence and assigning some different names to the spies -- a major text-critical issue discussed below.

5. **Verse 6 -- "Caleb son of Jephunneh" (כָּלֵב בֶּן־יְפֻנֶּה):** The name Kalev may derive from kelev (dog), though some scholars relate it to kal-lev (all-heart, wholehearted), which would anticipate the later divine commendation that Caleb had "a different spirit" and "followed fully" after YHWH (Numbers 14:24; 32:12). Jephunneh (יְפֻנֶּה) means "he will be turned" or "he will be faced." Caleb is identified elsewhere as a Kenizzite (Numbers 32:12; Joshua 14:6, 14), suggesting non-Israelite ancestry assimilated into the tribe of Judah. This makes his faithfulness all the more striking: the one whose loyalty is absolute is the one whose ethnic origin is marginal.

6. **Verse 8 -- "Hoshea son of Nun" (הוֹשֵׁעַ בִּן־נוּן):** Moses' attendant and successor is here called by his original name Hoshea (הוֹשֵׁעַ, "salvation"), not yet the form Yehoshua/Joshua. The significance of the name change is addressed at verse 16.

7. **Verse 16 -- "And Moses called Hoshea son of Nun, Joshua" (וַיִּקְרָא מֹשֶׁה לְהוֹשֵׁעַ בִּן־נוּן יְהוֹשֻׁעַ):** The renaming from Hoshea (salvation) to Yehoshua (YHWH saves, or YHWH is salvation) adds the theophoric yod-he prefix, incorporating the divine name into the personal name. This prefixing of the divine name transforms a general statement ("salvation") into a theological confession ("YHWH is salvation" or "YHWH will save"). The LXX renders this as Iesous (Ἰησοῦς) -- the same name later borne by Jesus of Nazareth in its Greek form. The placement of this renaming notice here, just before the spy mission begins, may indicate that Moses bestowed the name at this point as a kind of commissioning, or it may be a retrospective editorial note explaining that the Hoshea listed in verse 8 is in fact the well-known Joshua. The Talmud (Sotah 34b) interprets the name change as a prayer: "Yah save you from the counsel of the spies."

8. **Verse 17 -- "Go up this way into the Negev" (עֲלוּ זֶה בַּנֶּגֶב):** The demonstrative zeh (this) functions adverbially, meaning "this way" or "by this route." The Negev (literally "dry land" or "south") refers to the arid region south of the Judean hill country, the natural entry corridor from the Sinai into Canaan. The instruction to "go up" (alu) reflects the topography: ascending from the desert lowlands into the central hill country. The LXX renders αναβητε ταυτη τη ερημω (go up by this wilderness), substituting "wilderness" for "Negev." Moses' reconnaissance directives are methodical, covering population strength (v. 18), land quality (v. 19), agricultural productivity and defenses (v. 20), forming a comprehensive intelligence brief.

9. **Verse 19 -- "in camps or in fortifications" (הַבְּמַחֲנִים אִם בְּמִבְצָרִים):** The Hebrew machanayim (camps) versus mivtsarim (fortifications) presents a binary diagnostic: do the inhabitants live in unprotected encampments (suggesting nomadic or semi-nomadic weakness) or in fortified cities (suggesting organized military strength)? The LXX renders εν τειχηρεσιν η εν ατειχιστοις (in walled or unwalled cities). The distinction is strategically critical for an invading force: fortified cities require siege capability, while camps can be overrun by direct assault.

10. **Verse 20 -- "whether it is fertile or lean" (הַשְּׁמֵנָה הִוא אִם־רָזָה):** The adjective shemenah (fat, fertile, rich) and razah (lean, poor) describe soil quality using bodily metaphors. The LXX renders πιων η παρειμενη (fat or slack). The final clause -- "And the days were the days of the first-ripe grapes" (וְהַיָּמִים יְמֵי בִּכּוּרֵי עֲנָבִים) -- provides a temporal marker: the mission launched during the grape-ripening season, roughly late July to early August. This editorial note explains why the spies were able to bring back the extraordinary cluster of grapes (v. 23). The command "be strong" (vehithchazaqtem, Hithpael of חזק) is a military exhortation.

11. **Verse 21 -- "from the wilderness of Zin unto Rehob, at the entrance of Hamath" (מִמִּדְבַּר־צִן עַד־רְחֹב לְבֹא חֲמָת):** This describes the full north-south extent of the land: from the wilderness of Zin in the far south (distinct from the wilderness of Sin near Sinai) to Lebo-Hamath in the far north. Lebo-Hamath (literally "the entrance of Hamath") is typically identified with Lebweh in the Beqa Valley of modern Lebanon, marking the traditional northern boundary of the promised land (cf. Numbers 34:8; Joshua 13:5; Judges 3:3). The spies thus traversed the entire length of Canaan -- approximately two hundred and fifty miles -- a remarkable journey of intelligence gathering. The LXX renders εως Ρααβ εισπορευομενων εις Εμαθ (unto Raab entering into Emath).

12. **Verse 22 -- "And Ahiman, Sheshai, and Talmai, the descendants of Anak" (וְשָׁם אֲחִימַן שֵׁשַׁי וְתַלְמַי יְלִידֵי הָעֲנָק):** The yelidei ha-Anaq (descendants of Anak, literally "born ones of the Neck/Necklace") are the fearsome inhabitants of Hebron who will feature prominently in the spies' discouraging report. These three named individuals appear again in Joshua 15:14 and Judges 1:10, where Caleb drives them out. Anak (עֲנָק) may mean "long-necked" or "necklace-wearer," possibly referring to great stature. The parenthetical note about Hebron's antiquity -- "And Hebron was built seven years before Zoan of Egypt" (וְחֶבְרוֹן שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים נִבְנְתָה לִפְנֵי צֹעַן מִצְרָיִם) -- is a remarkable editorial insertion. Zoan (Tanis) was the administrative capital of the Egyptian Delta, one of the oldest and most prestigious cities of the ancient world. To say Hebron predates Zoan by seven years is to assert that this Canaanite city is of supreme antiquity, lending weight to both the importance and the difficulty of conquering it. The LXX preserves this chronological note.

13. **Verse 23 -- "a branch with one cluster of grapes" (זְמוֹרָה וְאֶשְׁכּוֹל עֲנָבִים אֶחָד):** The word zemorah (branch, vine-shoot) is from זמר (to prune); the branch was cut still bearing the cluster. The word eshkol (cluster) gives the valley its name (v. 24). That the cluster required two men carrying it on a mot (pole, bar) between them indicates extraordinary size. The LXX renders κληματος και βοτρυν σταφυλης (a vine-branch and a grape cluster). The specification "between two" (be-shnayim) has been interpreted variously: either one pole carried between two men, or two poles used to support the weight. Most translations follow the first reading. The addition of pomegranates and figs alongside the grapes represents the triad of agricultural abundance that characterizes the promised land (cf. Deuteronomy 8:8).

14. **Verse 24 -- "the Valley of Eshcol" (נַחַל אֶשְׁכּוֹל):** The etiological notice explains the place-name: Eshcol means "cluster." The nachal is a wadi or valley with a seasonal streambed. The LXX renders φαραγξ βοτρυος (Valley of the Cluster). The location is traditionally identified near Hebron, consistent with the geography of verse 22. The phrase "which the Sons of Israel cut down from there" attributes the action to the spies as representatives of the entire nation.

15. **Verse 25 -- "at the end of forty days" (מִקֵּץ אַרְבָּעִים יוֹם):** The forty-day duration will become theologically significant in 14:34, where YHWH sentences the people to forty years of wandering -- one year for each day of the spying mission. Forty is a typological number in the Hebrew Bible: the flood lasted forty days (Genesis 7:17), Moses was on Sinai forty days (Exodus 24:18; 34:28), and Elijah journeyed forty days to Horeb (1 Kings 19:8). The LXX reads τεσσαρακοντα ημερων (forty days).

16. **Verse 26 -- "to the wilderness of Paran, to Kadesh" (אֶל־מִדְבַּר פָּארָן קָדֵשָׁה):** Kadesh (Kadesh-barnea) is specified as the particular location within the larger wilderness of Paran where the camp was situated. The he-locale suffix on Kadesh-ah indicates direction ("toward Kadesh"). Kadesh-barnea is identified with modern Ein el-Qudeirat or Ein Qadis in the northeastern Sinai, and serves as the base camp for the Israelites during the critical events of Numbers 13-14. The verb "brought back word" (vayashivu otam davar) literally means "they returned to them a word" -- a report. The LXX renders εις Καδης (to Kadesh).

17. **Verse 27 -- "it flows with milk and honey" (זָבַת חָלָב וּדְבָשׁ):** The participial phrase zavat chalav u-devash (flowing with milk and honey) is the signature description of the promised land, occurring over twenty times in the Pentateuch (cf. Exodus 3:8, 17; 13:5; 33:3; Leviticus 20:24). "Milk" (chalav) signifies pastoral abundance (healthy flocks producing milk), while "honey" (devash) may refer to bee honey or, more likely, to the thick syrup of dates and figs, signifying agricultural fertility. Together they represent comprehensive abundance -- both pastoral and agricultural. The LXX renders ρεουσα γαλα και μελι (flowing with milk and honey). The spies' initial report is positive and accurate; the problem arises with the adversative "however" (ephes ki) in verse 28.

18. **Verse 28 -- "However" (אֶפֶס כִּי):** The particle ephes ki is a strong adversative meaning "however," "but," or "only that." It marks the turning point of the report from positive reconnaissance to fearful assessment. The people are az (strong, fierce), the cities are betsurot (fortified) and gedolot me'od (very great), and -- most alarming -- the yelidei ha-Anaq (descendants of Anak) are there. The LXX renders αλλ η οτι (but that). This single adversative conjunction transforms an encouraging report into a counsel of despair.

19. **Verse 29 -- The Ethnic Catalogue:** The spies enumerate five peoples occupying different geographic zones of Canaan: (a) Amalek in the Negev (the southern desert), (b) the Hittite, Jebusite, and Amorite in the hill country (the central highland ridge), and (c) the Canaanite by the sea (the Mediterranean coast) and along the Jordan (the rift valley). This is a military assessment: the land is defended on every front. The Amalek blocks the southern approach, the highland peoples hold the strategic heights, and the Canaanites control the coastal plain and the eastern corridor. The LXX follows the same enumeration. The Amalekites are Israel's perpetual enemies since Exodus 17:8-16; the Jebusites hold Jerusalem until David's conquest (2 Samuel 5:6-9); the Amorites are the dominant highland population; the Hittites represent remnants of the great Hittite empire or a local Canaanite group bearing the same name.

20. **Verse 30 -- "And Caleb silenced the people before Moses" (וַיַּהַס כָּלֵב אֶת־הָעָם אֶל־מֹשֶׁה):** The verb hissah (Hiphil of הסה) means to silence, to still, to hush. Caleb's intervention is dramatic: the people are in growing agitation, and he imposes silence. The phrase el-Mosheh (before Moses, or toward Moses) may mean he directed his words to Moses, or that he calmed the people in the direction of Moses (i.e., in Moses' defense). The LXX renders κατεσιωπησεν Χαλεβ τον λαον (Caleb silenced the people). His declaration "let us surely go up" (aloh na'aleh) uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis -- "going up, let us go up" -- followed by "and possess it" (veyarashnu otah). The verb yarash (to possess, to dispossess) is the key conquest verb of Deuteronomy. The phrase "we are well able" (yakol nukhal) repeats the root יכל (to be able) emphatically: "being able, we are able." Caleb's response is pure faith: he does not deny the strength of the inhabitants but affirms that Israel's ability (grounded in YHWH's promise) exceeds it.

21. **Verse 31 -- "they are stronger than we" (כִּי־חָזָק הוּא מִמֶּנּוּ):** The other spies' rebuttal is terse: "we are not able" (lo nukhal) directly negates Caleb's emphatic nukhal. The singular hu (he) in "he is stronger than we" likely refers collectively to "the people" (ha-am) as a singular entity. Some rabbinic interpreters (Sotah 35a) read mimmennu ambiguously as either "than we" or "than him" -- suggesting the blasphemous claim that the inhabitants are stronger even than God. The LXX renders ισχυει μαλλον η ημεις (he is stronger than we).

22. **Verse 32 -- "an evil report" (דִּבַּת הָאָרֶץ רָעָה):** The word dibbah means report, rumor, or slander -- always with negative connotation in the Hebrew Bible (cf. Genesis 37:2; Proverbs 10:18; 25:10). The phrase dibbat ha-arets ra'ah (an evil report of the land) characterizes their words as not merely pessimistic but morally culpable. The description of Canaan as "a land that devours its inhabitants" (erets okhelet yoshveha) is a striking metaphor: the land itself consumes those who live in it. This may refer to constant warfare between the various peoples, to disease or difficult conditions, or it may be hyperbolic fearmongering. The LXX renders εκσταsin (horror/astonishment) for the report. The phrase "men of great stature" (anshei middot) literally means "men of measures" -- men of extraordinary dimensions.

23. **Verse 33 -- "And there we saw the Nephilim" (וְשָׁם רָאִינוּ אֶת־הַנְּפִלִים):** The Nephilim appear only here and in Genesis 6:4, where they are described as the mighty ones of old, the men of renown, associated with the union of the "sons of God" and the "daughters of man." The parenthetical gloss -- "the sons of Anak, who come from the Nephilim" (בְּנֵי עֲנָק מִן־הַנְּפִלִים) -- identifies the Anakim as a subset or lineage of the Nephilim. The claim is extraordinary and arguably exaggerated: the Nephilim were pre-flood beings (Genesis 6:4), and their presence in Canaan is either a survival tradition or -- more likely in context -- part of the deliberate exaggeration that characterizes the "evil report." The LXX renders τους γιγαντας (the giants). The self-comparison "we were in our own eyes as grasshoppers" (וַנְּהִי בְעֵינֵינוּ כַּחֲגָבִים) uses the smallest of the clean insects (Leviticus 11:22) as an image of utter insignificance. The final clause -- "and so we were in their eyes" (וְכֵן הָיִינוּ בְּעֵינֵיהֶם) -- projects their own self-perception onto the enemy, a psychological detail of remarkable precision: those who see themselves as grasshoppers assume their enemies see them the same way.

### LXX Divergences

The Septuagint text of Numbers 13 in Codex Vaticanus presents one of the most significant divergences from the Masoretic Text in the entire Pentateuch. The LXX differs in: (a) the ordering of the tribal list -- the LXX follows a different sequence for the twelve spies; (b) some of the names are spelled differently or represent variant transliterations; (c) the LXX expands certain phrases with clarifying additions. The Vaticanus manuscript (pa_0121 to pa_0122) is continuous uncial script with no word spacing, requiring careful parsing of the Greek. The chapter falls at the transition between two columns on pa_0121 and continues onto pa_0122, where the chapter heading marker for Numbers 14 (ΚΣ = chapter 26 in the LXX numbering) is visible on the left margin. The overall narrative structure and theological content remain consistent between the MT and LXX despite these textual variations.

### Structural Observations

- Numbers 13 forms the first half of the great spy narrative (13-14), which is one of the pivotal episodes in the entire Pentateuch. The chapter divides into five sections: the divine commission (vv. 1-3), the roster of spies (vv. 4-16), Moses' instructions (vv. 17-20), the journey and discoveries (vv. 21-24), and the report with its division (vv. 25-33). The structural center is Moses' detailed reconnaissance instructions (vv. 17-20), which establish the criteria by which the spies will assess the land -- and by which the reader will assess the spies.

- The chapter is framed by two acts of naming: Moses names Joshua (v. 16) and the spies name the Valley of Eshcol (v. 24). The first naming is an act of faith (YHWH saves); the second is an act of witness (the land is abundant). Together they embody the two elements needed for conquest: divine power and a desirable goal. The tragedy of chapter 14 is that the people possess the evidence (the fruit) and the leadership (Joshua and Caleb) but lack the faith.

- The twelve spies mirror the twelve tribes in a representative function: as the spies respond, so will the nation respond. Ten spies bring a faithless report, and correspondingly the majority of the nation rebels. Two spies bring a faithful report, and correspondingly a faithful remnant will eventually enter the land. The ratio of ten to two anticipates the recurring biblical pattern of a faithful minority against an unfaithful majority.

- The "evil report" (dibbat ha-arets ra'ah, v. 32) is a literary and theological concept, not merely a military assessment. The spies do not simply convey negative information; they slander the land that YHWH has promised. Their report contradicts verse 27 (flowing with milk and honey) with verse 32 (a land that devours its inhabitants) -- the same land cannot be both. The internal contradiction exposes the report as distorted by fear. The ten spies saw the same land as Caleb and Joshua but interpreted it through the lens of their own inadequacy rather than YHWH's sufficiency.

- The progression from "we are not able" (v. 31) to "a land that devours" (v. 32) to "we were as grasshoppers" (v. 33) traces an escalation of faithlessness: from tactical doubt (we cannot defeat them) to cosmic terror (the land itself is lethal) to existential collapse (we are nothing). Each stage moves further from the concrete military data Moses requested and deeper into psychological and spiritual despair. The report that began with accurate intelligence (v. 27-28) deteriorates into fantasy and self-annihilation (v. 33).

- The parenthetical note about Hebron's founding seven years before Zoan (v. 22) and the etiological note about the Valley of Eshcol (v. 24) are narrative pauses that give the land historical and cultural depth. They remind the reader that the promised land is not an empty abstraction but a real place with ancient cities, established populations, and remarkable fertility -- all of which both confirms its desirability and explains the fear of those who must conquer it.
